author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Pelagius’ Own Account of the Faculties, Quoted.
title: Pelagius’ Own Account of the Faculties, Quoted.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Chapter 5 [IV.]—Pelagius’ Own Account of the Faculties, Quoted.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Lest, however, it should chance to be said that we either do not correctly understand what he advances, or malevolently pervert to another meaning what he never meant to bear such a sense, I beg of you to consider his own actual words: “We distinguish,” says he, “three things, arranging them in a certain graduated order.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: We put in the first place ‘ability;’ in the second, ‘volition;’ and in the third, ‘actuality.’

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: The ‘ability’ we place in our nature, the ‘volition’ in our will, and the ‘actuality’ in the effect.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: The first, that is, the ‘ability,’ properly belongs to God, who has bestowed it on His creature; the other two, that is, the ‘volition’ and the ‘actuality,’ must be referred to man, because they flow forth from the fountain of the will.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For his willing, therefore, and doing a good work, the praise belongs to man; or rather both to man, and to God who has bestowed on him the ‘capacity’ for his will and work, and who evermore by the help of His grace assists even this capacity.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: That a man is able to will and effect any good work, comes from God alone.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: So that this one faculty can exist, even when the other two have no being; but these latter cannot exist without that former one.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: I am therefore free not to have either a good volition or action; but I am by no means able not to have the capacity of good.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: This capacity is inherent in me, whether I will or no; nor does nature at any time receive in this point freedom for itself.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Now the meaning of all this will be rendered clearer by an example or two.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: That we are able to see with our eyes is not of us; but it is our own that we make a good or a bad use of our eyes.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: So again (that I may, by applying a general case in illustration, embrace all), that we are able to do, say, think, any good thing, comes from Him who has endowed us with this ‘ability,’ and who also assists this ‘ability;’ but that we really do a good thing, or speak a good word, or think a good thought, proceeds from our own selves, because we are also able to turn all these into evil.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Accordingly,—and this is a point which needs frequent repetition, because of your calumniation of us,—whenever we say that a man can live without sin, we also give praise to God by our acknowledgment of the capacity which we have received from Him, who has bestowed such ‘ability’ upon us; and there is here no occasion for praising the human agent, since it is God’s matter alone that is for the moment treated of; for the question is not about ‘willing,’ or ‘effecting,’ but simply and solely about that which may possibly be.”
